one_line_takeaway: SafeProbing detects jailbreak success mid-generation by measuring, via a small amount of LoRA fine-tuning, how strongly the model's own next-token probabilities agree with an appended 'illegal and unethical' disclaimer, achieving higher defense success (95.1% avg DSR on Qwen, 98.2% on Mistral) than eight baselines while preserving utility (near-zero GSM/JustEval degradation) and cutting over-refusals versus other detection-based defenses.
key_finding: On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SafeProbing achieves the highest average DSR across the nine harmful-prompt/attack categories reported in Table 1 (95.1% avg), ahead of the next-best baseline SelfEval (80.1% avg) and Backtranslation (85.5% avg); on Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 it reaches 98.2% avg vs. SelfEval's 93.6% and Backtranslation's 75.1%. Against the hardest individual attacks, SafeProbing shows large margins over No Defense, e.g. on Qwen: DRA rises from 5.6% (No Defense) to 95.6%, AutoDAN-Turbo from 26.7% to 96.0%, ReNeLLM from 20.4% to 87.9%; on Mistral: DRA rises from 15.6% to 98.3%, AutoDAN-Turbo from 15.2% to 99.0%. Utility is largely preserved: on Qwen, GSM accuracy is unchanged (0.81, -0%) and JustEval average is 4.63 vs. 4.64 for No Defense (-0%), compared to sharp utility drops for competing methods (e.g., RobustAligned: GSM -62% on Qwen, -95% on Mistral; SmoothLLM: GSM -48% on Qwen, -69% on Mistral). Over-refusal on XSTest is lower than the other detection-based baselines: 18 (Qwen) and 11 (Mistral) for SafeProbing vs. 165/193 for RobustAligned, 62/65 for SelfEval, and 37/57 for Backtranslation (Figure 5). Ablations show in-decoding probing (ratio>0) is essential: on Qwen against AutoDAN-Turbo, ratio=0 (probe only after full generation) yields DSR=28.8% vs. 96.0% at ratio=0.20; even a small ratio of 0.05 already reaches most of the gain (93.1% on AutoDAN-Turbo). The safety-awareness LoRA fine-tuning materially helps: at tau=2.0, the untrained model reaches 90.3% avg DSR / 23 over-refusals on Qwen, while the trained SafeProbing reaches 93.1% avg DSR / 18 over-refusals. Among tested disclaimer clauses, "illegal and unethical" gives the strongest separation (mean L_disc=0.58 for harmful content under AutoDAN) versus weaker alternatives like "not allowed and harmful" (mean 4.39).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from cited work in Section 2) is SafeProbing: a jailbreak defense that exploits an observed phenomenon in which LLMs, after being successfully jailbroken into generating harmful content, sometimes append an unprompted safety disclaimer -- evidence that the model retains a latent, suppressed 'safety-awareness' during decoding that is overridden by its drive for fluent continuation. Building on this observation, the authors (1) devise a probing signal: append a fixed neutral trigger phrase ('Note that this is') to the in-progress generation and measure the model's negative log-likelihood of continuing with a core disclaimer clause ('illegal and unethical'), where low loss indicates the model implicitly 'agrees' the content is harmful; (2) lightly fine-tune the model with LoRA on a small subset of SafeRLHF (binary harmful/harmless labels) plus UltraFeedback (as an instruction-following regularizer) using an MSE loss on a smoothed/bounded transform of this disclaimer loss, to widen the separation between benign and harmful loss distributions; and (3) apply this probe at randomly sampled in-decoding checkpoints (20% of steps in the main experiments) during generation itself, rather than only after the full response is produced, halting generation and returning a fixed refusal string if the loss at any checkpoint falls below a threshold tau (tau=2.0 used).
threat_model: Adversary submits jailbreak-crafted prompts (AutoDAN, PAIR, IFSJ, ReNeLLM, REDA, DRA, AutoDAN-Turbo, and an encoded/code-hiding style attack shown in Figure 2) to an aligned, deployed LLM in order to elicit harmful content (e.g., bomb-making instructions, scam/fraud instructions, database-hacking content) that bypasses the model's safety alignment. The defense is a runtime, model-side intervention operating during decoding (white-box access to token probabilities is assumed, since the defense computes the model's own next-token loss on an appended probe phrase); it does not assume knowledge of the specific attack technique used to craft the prompt.
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors in the Limitations section: the current implementation always returns a fixed, conservative refusal string once harmful content is detected, rather than a more flexible response (e.g., partial redaction, guided rephrasing, safety-aware continuation); this was a deliberate choice to isolate the detection capability rather than optimize end-user experience, deferred to future work. Additional limitations evident from the paper (not necessarily flagged by the authors as limitations): the method requires white-box access to per-token model log-probabilities, so it cannot be applied to closed/API-only models without such access; it requires LoRA fine-tuning with labeled harmful/harmless data (SafeRLHF) per target model, plus per-model tuning of the sharpness hyperparameter beta and threshold tau; only two models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) are evaluated in the main text captured here, with broader-model and GPT-Judge-scored harmfulness results relegated to an appendix not captured in this extraction; and the in-decoding probing adds inference-time computational overhead (quantified only in an appendix not captured here) from repeated forward passes to compute the disclaimer loss at sampled checkpoints.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) - 520 harmful prompts, used both as source for attack construction and as a direct harmful-prompt benchmark
SafeRLHF (Dai et al., 2024) - small subset used with binary harmful/harmless labels for LoRA fine-tuning
UltraFeedback (Cui et al., 2023) - small proportion mixed into training batches as instruction-following regularization
GSM (Cobbe et al., 2021) - math ability benchmark for utility evaluation
JustEval (Lin et al., 2023) - 800-instruction benchmark judged by GPT-4.1 on Helpfulness/Clarity/Factuality/Depth/Engagement
XSTest (Röttger et al., 2024) - benign-but-sensitive prompts used to measure over-refusal
MM-SafetyBench, FigStep, HADES - multimodal jailbreak benchmarks used in a preliminary multimodal extension (quantitative results in an appendix not captured in this extraction)
quantitative_results: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, average Defense Success Rate across 9 categories in Table 1: SafeProbing 95.1% vs. No Defense 31.0%, Backtranslation 85.5%, SelfEval 80.1%, RobustAligned 62.3%
Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, average DSR: SafeProbing 98.2% vs. No Defense 12.3%, SelfEval 93.6%, Backtranslation 75.1%
Qwen DRA attack DSR: No Defense 5.6% -> SafeProbing 95.6%; Mistral DRA: No Defense 15.6% -> SafeProbing 98.3%
Qwen AutoDAN-Turbo DSR: No Defense 26.7% -> SafeProbing 96.0%
Qwen GSM accuracy: No Defense 0.81, SafeProbing 0.81 (-0%), vs. RobustAligned 0.31 (-62%), SmoothLLM 0.42 (-48%)
Mistral GSM accuracy: No Defense 0.49, SafeProbing 0.49 (-0%), vs. RobustAligned 0.02 (-95%), SmoothLLM 0.15 (-69%)
JustEval average score (1-5): Qwen No Defense 4.64 vs SafeProbing 4.63 (-0%); Mistral No Defense 4.38 vs SafeProbing 4.33 (-1%)
XSTest over-refusal counts (Figure 5): SafeProbing 18 (Qwen) / 11 (Mistral) vs. RobustAligned 165/193, SelfEval 62/65, Backtranslation 37/57
Ablation on in-decoding ratio (Table 4), Qwen vs. AutoDAN-Turbo: ratio=0 -> DSR 28.8%; ratio=0.05 -> 93.1%; ratio=0.20 -> 96.0%

Ablation on threshold tau without training (Table 3), Qwen avg across 3 attacks: tau=1.0 -> 75.1% DSR/10 over-refusals; tau=2.0 -> 90.3% DSR/23 over-refusals; with training at tau=2.0 -> 93.1% DSR/18 over-refusals
Density distributions (Figure 3) on original Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: mean L_disc = 8.34 (benign, last-check) vs 0.58 (harmful, in-decoding), 5.58 (benign, in-decoding), 3.07 (harmful, last-check)
Disclaimer clause ablation (Table 5), Qwen under AutoDAN: mean L_disc = 0.58 for "illegal and unethical" vs. 4.39 for "not allowed and harmful"
baselines_compared: ICD (Wei et al., 2023) - in-context refusal demonstrations
SafeDecoding (Xu et al., 2024) - token distribution mixing with an expert model
SmoothLLM (Robey et al., 2025) - random prompt perturbation
DRO (Zheng et al., 2024a) - trained continuous soft prompts
Backtranslation (Wang et al., 2024) - infer user's likely malicious intent from the response
RobustAligned (Cao et al., 2024) - randomized input dropout
SelfEval (Phute et al., 2024) - self-assessment of own response harmfulness
